1: At that time (bāʿēṯ hahîʾ) Jeroboam’s son Abijah became ill. The notice connects this episode to the preceding material — the “that time” is the period of Jeroboam’s consolidation of the northern apostasy. The illness of the crown prince is the occasion for Jeroboam’s consultation of the prophet Ahijah — the same prophet who had announced Jeroboam’s election (1 Kings 11:29-39). The king who rejected YHWH’s covenant worship still turns to YHWH’s prophet in crisis; the pattern of selective religious use (consulting YHWH only when personally in need) is a form of the covenant violation it accompanies.
2: Jeroboam instructs his wife to disguise herself so no one will know she is Jeroboam’s wife, then go to Shiloh to consult Ahijah. The disguise (hišṯannê, from šānāh, H8138, to change/disguise) is Jeroboam’s attempt to conceal his identity from the prophet — as if the prophet’s knowledge were merely human. The choice of Shiloh is significant: the ancient covenant sanctuary where the ark rested (1 Sam 1-4), now superseded by the Jerusalem temple and Jeroboam’s Bethel-Dan installations. Jeroboam consults the authentic YHWH-prophet at the authentic covenant site while maintaining his own false cult elsewhere — the contradiction revealing his theological duplicity.
3: The wife takes ten loaves, some cakes, and a jar of honey — a modest consultation gift (cf. 1 Samuel 9:7-8 where a quarter shekel was given to Samuel). The specific provisions — lōḥem ûniqquḏîm ûḇaqqbuqiṯ dĕḇaš — are ordinary food items, not royal gifts. The disguise extends to the modest gift: by sending ordinary hospitality-provisions rather than royal gifts, Jeroboam attempts to present his wife as a common petitioner. Ahijah will reveal the identity regardless — his blindness makes the disguise irrelevant to him; his prophetic sight makes it irrelevant to YHWH.
4: Jeroboam’s wife came to Ahijah’s house in Shiloh. Ahijah could not see because his eyes were set (kāmû ʿênāyw) from old age. The blindness of the old prophet — established before his identification of Jeroboam’s wife — is the narrative preparation for the supernatural recognition in 1 Kings 14:6. The physical blindness contrasts with the prophetic sight: Ahijah cannot see with eyes but YHWH had already told him who was coming. The same dynamic recurs with the blind Isaac in Gen 27 (where the blindness created an opportunity for deception) — here it creates an opportunity for demonstration of prophetic (YHWH-given) knowledge over against human (disguise-based) concealment.
5: But YHWH had told Ahijah: “The wife of Jeroboam is coming to you — she will ask you about her son, who is sick.” YHWH’s preemptive disclosure to the prophet is the standard prophetic preparation narrative (cf. 1 Samuel 9:15-16 where YHWH told Samuel about Saul the day before his arrival). The prophet who has been told in advance receives the disguised visitor with full knowledge, making the disguise irrelevant. The command to Ahijah — kōh wĕḵōh tĕdabbēr ʾēlêhā (say this and this to her) — shows the prophetic oracle as YHWH’s direct speech through the prophet, not the prophet’s independent composition.
6: wayyishmaʿ ʾăḥiyyāh ʾeṯ-qôl raglêhā bāʾāh bappetaḥ — Ahijah heard her footstep at the door. The prophet’s hearing in place of sight is the DtrH’s subtle irony: the blind prophet hears what the disguise cannot conceal. His greeting — “Come in, wife of Jeroboam. Why do you pretend to be someone else (lāmmāh zōʾ miṯnaḵrîy)?” — penetrates the disguise immediately. naḵar in miṯnaḵrîy (H5234 Hithpael, to act as a stranger, disguise oneself) is the verb used for Joseph pretending to be a stranger to his brothers (Genesis 42:7). The question “why do you disguise yourself?” exposes the futility of concealment before YHWH’s prophet.
7: lēḵ ʾimrî lĕyārāḇʿām — Go, say to Jeroboam: thus says YHWH the God of Israel. The oracle begins with YHWH’s covenant title and his acts of election: “I raised you up (ʾerimmōm, H7311 Polel, to exalt/lift up) from among the people and made you nāgîḏ over my people Israel.” nāgîḏ (H5057, appointed leader/ruler) is the election vocabulary: YHWH appointed Saul (1 Samuel 9:16; 1 Kings 10:1), David (2 Samuel 5:2; 1 Kings 7:8), and Solomon (1 Chronicles 29:22) as nāgîḏ — the designation of the one whom YHWH has specifically chosen from among the people for covenant leadership. The contrast between YHWH’s generous election and Jeroboam’s response will structure 1 Kings 14:8-9.
8: wāʾeqraʿ ʾeṯ-hammamlāḵāh mibêṯ dāwid wāʾettĕnāh lāḵ wĕlōʾ-hāyîṯā kĕʿaḇdî dāwid — I tore the kingdom from the house of David and gave it to you; yet you have not been like my servant David. The contrast is explicit: YHWH gave the kingdom to Jeroboam as a gift; David is the covenant standard against which Jeroboam is measured. ʿaḇdî dāwid (my servant David) is the highest covenant designation — David kept my commandments and walked after me with all his heart (bĕḵol-lĕḇāḇô, H3824) doing only what was right in my eyes. The Deuteronomic standard of wholehearted covenant loyalty (kol-lēḇāḇ) is the baseline Jeroboam has violated.
9: wayyaʿaś rāʿōṯ mikkōl ʾăšer hāyû lĕpānêḵā wayyēleḵ wayyaʿaś lĕḵā ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm ûmaṣṣēḵôṯ lĕhakʿisēnî — you have done more evil than all who were before you, making for yourself other gods and cast images, to provoke me to anger. hakkĕʿîs (H3707, to provoke to jealousy/anger) is the technical term for covenant-violation through idolatry (Deuteronomy 31:29; 1 Kings 32:21). The golden calves are classified as ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm (other gods) — not merely representations of YHWH but functionally equivalent to the foreign gods that the covenant prohibits. wayyašlēḵ ʾōṯî ʾaḥăreḵā (you cast me behind your back) — the idiom for contemptuous dismissal, as if YHWH were an inconvenient obligation to be discarded.
10: lāḵēn hinnî mēḇîʾ rāʿāh ʾel-bêṯ yārāḇʿām — therefore I am bringing disaster on the house of Jeroboam. The hinnênî (behold me/I am) formula introduces direct divine action. The dynastic curse formula — “I will cut off from Jeroboam every male, slave or free in Israel” (kol-māgûr ûsāḡûr bĕyiśrāʾēl) — is the technical language for total dynastic elimination. māgûr ûsāḡûr is an idiomatic pair meaning everyone without exception (cf. the same formula in Deuteronomy 32:36; 1 Kings 21:21). “As one burns dung until it is gone” (ûḇiʿartî ʾaḥărê bêṯ yārāḇʿām kaʾăšer yĕbaʿer haggālāl ʾaḏ-tummô) — the dung-burning metaphor for complete, undignified elimination of the dynasty.
11: Dogs will eat those of Jeroboam who die in the city; birds of the air will eat those who die in the countryside. The double curse — dogs in the city, birds in the field — is the covenant curse of unburied death (Deuteronomy 28:26: “your carcass shall be food for all birds of the air and for the beasts of the earth”). The denial of proper burial is the severest expression of covenant judgment. The formula applies to Jeroboam’s entire household — the same fate announced for Ahab’s house (1 Kings 21:24) and for the house of Baasha (1 Kings 16:4) — suggesting a standard DtrH formula for dynasties under covenant judgment.
12: qûmî lĕḵî lĕḇêṯēḵ bĕḇôʾ raglayiḵ hāʿîrāh wāmēṯ hayyāleḏ — get up and go home. When your feet enter the city, the child will die. The immediately fulfilled sign-prediction (the child dying when she crosses the threshold) is the authenticating portent for the longer-term dynasty-doom oracle. The child’s death is simultaneously punishment (on the house of Jeroboam) and mercy (on the child himself, as 1 Kings 14:13 explains). The threshold-crossing is a precise trigger — the moment she re-enters Jeroboam’s household, the child dies. The child is the last good thing in the house of Jeroboam; his death removes it.
13: wĕhûʾ lĕḇaddô yiqqāḇēr lĕyārāḇʿām — he alone of Jeroboam’s house will come to burial (lĕ qĕḇûrāh). The explanation: “in him alone, in the house of Jeroboam, was found something good (daḇar ṭôḇ) before YHWH the God of Israel.” dāḇār ṭôḇ (a good word/thing) — the precise content is unstated but the structural point is clear: among all the members of Jeroboam’s household, only this child retained some genuine covenant orientation. The proper burial is the covenant mercy extended specifically to the one in whom good was found. YHWH’s judicial precision extends to individuals within a condemned dynasty.
14: wĕhēqîm YHWH lô meleḵ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer yakrît ʾeṯ-bêṯ-yārāḇʿām — YHWH will raise up for himself a king over Israel who will cut off the house of Jeroboam. The announcement of the dynasty’s divine replacement uses the same election vocabulary as Jeroboam’s own appointment (hēqîm, H6965, to raise up — the covenant appointment formula). The king YHWH will raise up is Baasha (1 Kings 15:27-30), who fulfills this oracle by killing Jeroboam’s son Nadab and eliminating the entire dynasty. The pattern: YHWH appoints, dynasty fails, YHWH raises up a new dynasty — the cycle that characterizes the northern kingdom’s history until the Assyrian conquest.
15: wĕhikkāh YHWH ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl kaʾăšer yānûd hāqāneh bammāyim — YHWH will strike Israel as a reed is shaken in the water. The simile of the water-reed (qāneh bammāyim) for Israel’s instability is a powerful image: the reed is rooted but bends completely with every current, having no stability of its own. wĕnāṯaš ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl mēʿal hāʾădāmāh haṭṭôḇāh — he will uproot Israel from this good land. The “good land” (hāʾădāmāh haṭṭôḇāh) is the covenant inheritance — YHWH’s gift to Israel at the conquest. The uprooting from the good land anticipates the Assyrian exile (722 BCE) — Ahijah is prophesying the northern kingdom’s end approximately 200 years before it occurs.
16: wĕyitēn ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl bĕgĕlal ḥaṭṭōʾṯ yārāḇʿām — he will abandon Israel because of the sins of Jeroboam. The causal connection between Jeroboam’s foundational sins and the exile of the northern kingdom is the DtrH’s central theological claim about the north. The phrase “who sinned and who caused Israel to sin” (ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ wăʾăšer heḥĕṭîʾ ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl) — Jeroboam as both personal sinner and national sin-instigator — will become the standard formula applied to virtually every northern king (1 Kings 15:26,30,34; 1 Kings 16:2,19,26; 1 Kings 22:52; 2 Kings 3:3; 1 Kings 10:29; etc.). The formula establishes Jeroboam as the paradigmatic covenant-violator against whom all northern kings are measured.
17: Jeroboam’s wife rose and left for Tirzah (tirṣāh) — the new royal city replacing Shechem/Penuel as Jeroboam’s capital. As she crossed the threshold (kĕḇōʾāh bĕsap-habbayyiṯ), the child died. The precise fulfillment of the threshold-trigger (1 Kings 14:12) is reported without embellishment: word-event correspondence at the moment specified. Tirzah (tirṣāh, H8656) is likely Tell el-Farʿah North, a major Iron Age site in the Samarian highlands. Its name means “pleasantness/favor” — the irony of a city whose name evokes divine pleasure being the site of the dynasty’s judgment-fulfillment.
18: They buried him and all Israel mourned for him, as YHWH had said through his servant Ahijah the prophet. The fulfillment notice — kĕḏĕḇar YHWH ʾăšer dibbēr bĕyaḏ-ʿaḇdô ʾăḥiyyāh hannaḇîʾ (according to the word of YHWH which he spoke through his servant Ahijah the prophet) — is the DtrH’s standard prophetic fulfillment formula. The child receives the mourning of all Israel (wəkol-yiśrāʾēl yisspĕḏû-lô) and proper burial — the covenant honor that the rest of Jeroboam’s dynasty will be denied. The fulfillment of the specific prediction validates the wider oracle against the dynasty.
19: The rest of the acts of Jeroboam — how he made war and how he reigned — are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel (sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lĕmalkê yiśrāʾēl). The reference to the “Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel” is the DtrH’s standard citation formula for the official court annals — a royal archive now lost. The citation validates the historical claims while acknowledging that the DtrH is selective in its presentation, focusing on theological evaluation rather than comprehensive history.
20: Jeroboam reigned twenty-two years (ʿeśrîm ûšĕtayim šānāh) and rested with his ancestors (wayyiškāḇ ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw). The “rested with his ancestors” formula is the standard DtrH death notice. The length of Jeroboam’s reign — 22 years — is long for a northern king; most of the northern dynasty successions are violent and brief. His son Nadab succeeded him. The transition note is brief because the dynasty is already under the divine sentence announced in 1 Kings 14:10-16; Nadab will be killed by Baasha in his second year (1 Kings 15:25-27).
21: Rehoboam son of Solomon reigned in Judah — forty-one years old at accession, seventeen-year reign, in Jerusalem the city YHWH had chosen from all the tribes of Israel to put his name there (lāśûm ʾeṯ-šĕmô šām). The šēm (Name) theology is the DtrH’s central formulation of the Deuteronomic presence-theology: YHWH does not dwell physically in the temple but causes his name to dwell there (Deuteronomy 12:5,11,21; 1 Kings 8:29). His mother was Naamah the Ammonite (nāʿămāh hāʿammônîṯ) — Solomon’s foreign wife whose son now reigns over Judah. The Ammonite queen-mother is a notable datum: the Davidic dynasty’s heir is half-Ammonite, from exactly the foreign-wife marriages that 1 Kgs 11 condemned.
22: wayyaʿaś yĕhûḏāh hārāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH wayyaqnĕʾûhû miqqōl ʾăšer ʿāśû ʾăḇōṯām bĕḥaṭṭōʾṯêhem — Judah did what was evil in YHWH’s eyes; with their sins they provoked him to jealousy more than all their ancestors had done. qānāʾ (H7065, to be jealous/provoke to jealousy) is the covenant-marriage term: YHWH’s relationship with Israel is covenantally spousal, and idolatry is adultery against the covenant spouse. “More than all their ancestors had done” is a superlative of covenant faithlessness — each generation’s apostasy is measured against the entire preceding tradition.
23: High places (bāmôṯ), sacred pillars (maṣṣēḇôṯ), and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) on every high hill and under every spreading tree. The triad of Canaanite cultic installations — high place, standing stone, Asherah pole — represents the full program of indigenous Levantine religion that Deuteronomy prohibited (Deuteronomy 7:5; 1 Kings 12:3; 1 Kings 16:21-22). The “every high hill and spreading tree” (kol-gibʿāh gĕḇōhāh wĕtaḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān) is the DtrH’s formulaic description of Canaanite sanctuary topography — the same formula used for Israel’s pervasive cultic apostasy throughout Kings and the prophets (cf. Jeremiah 2:20; 1 Kings 3:6; Ezekiel 6:13).
24: wĕgam-qāḏēš hāyāh bāʾāreṣ — there were also qĕḏēšîm in the land. qāḏēš (H6945, consecrated person, male cult prostitute) designates the shrine-functionary who participated in Canaanite fertility rituals. The DtrH regards this as the most extreme marker of Canaanite assimilation: “they did all the abominations (ḵol-tôʿăḇōṯ haggerôyim) of the nations which YHWH drove out before the Israelites.” tôʿēḇāh (H8441, abomination) is the strongest DtrH term for proscribed practices — the cult prostitution represents complete assimilation to what the Canaanite dispossession was designed to eliminate from the land.
25: In Rehoboam’s fifth year, Shishak (šîšaq) king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem. šîšaq is Shoshenq I (Sheshonq I), founder of Egypt’s 22nd dynasty (ruled ca. 945-924 BCE) — the first externally corroborated event in the Kings narrative (corroborated by Shoshenq’s own victory inscription at Karnak, which lists over 150 conquered cities including sites in both Judah and Israel). His campaign (ca. 926-925 BCE) is a historically verifiable event that anchors the DtrH chronology in Egyptian records. Rehoboam’s fifth year = ca. 926 BCE.
26: wayyiqqaḥ ʾeṯ-ʾōṣrôṯ bêṯ-YHWH wĕʾeṯ-ʾōṣrôṯ bêṯ-hammelek hakkōl lāqāḥ — he took the treasures of the house of YHWH and the treasures of the royal palace — he took everything. The formula “he took everything” (hakkōl lāqāḥ) is the DtrH’s verdict on total plunder. The golden shields that Solomon had made — 300 large shields, 200 small (1 Kings 10:16-17) — are included in the plunder. The golden shields were ceremonial objects of Solomonic wealth; their removal begins the visible deterioration of Davidic royal splendor.
27: In their place Rehoboam made bronze shields (māginnê nĕḥōšeṯ) and gave them to the commanders of the palace guard. The substitution of bronze for gold is the DtrH’s most economical symbol of Judah’s covenantal diminishment: the gold of Solomon’s kingdom replaced by the bronze of a lesser king. The visual image is deliberate — bronze looks like gold from a distance but is worth far less. The reduced kingdom maintains the forms of Solomonic glory without the substance. The shield substitution will become a recurring image in the Kings narrative for the gap between covenant promise and covenant reality.
28: Whenever the king went to the house of YHWH, the guard carried the shields and then returned them to the guard room. The ceremonial function of the bronze shields — carried in procession, returned to storage — preserves the ritual form of the Solomonic guard-procession while demonstrating the diminishment of its material. The DtrH records this detail because it captures the entire Rehoboam period: the form of the Solomonic covenant maintained, the substance diminished. Cultic continuity in the midst of covenantal decline is a persistent DtrH theme.
29: The rest of Rehoboam’s acts are written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah (sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lĕmalkê yĕhûḏāh). The Judean chronicle citation parallels the Israelite citation in 1 Kings 14:19, establishing the DtrH’s dual source-protocol for northern (Israel) and southern (Judah) kings. The Judean chronicles are a separate archive from the Israelite chronicles — distinct royal administrations maintaining their own records from the division onward.
30: There was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days (kol-hayyāmîm). The summary of continuous warfare between the divided kingdoms is the DtrH’s indictment of the division’s consequences: the covenant brother-kingdoms are perpetually at war. The warfare consumes the military resources that Solomon had used against external enemies; the north and south bleed each other while external threats (Shishak) exploit the weakness. The summary frames the period as one of unrelieved covenant-dysfunction.
31: Rehoboam rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David (ʿîr dāwid). The burial in the city of David — Jerusalem, the Davidic capital — is the standard honorable burial for Judahite kings, contrasting with the denials of burial that the DtrH records for apostate dynasties. His mother’s name is given again: Naamah the Ammonite. The DtrH’s repetition of the Ammonite-queen-mother datum (cf. 1 Kings 14:21) frames Rehoboam’s reign within the problematic foreign-marriage legacy of Solomon. His son Abijam succeeded him — the Davidic dynasty continues despite the covenant failures.
